name: Luxembourg
summary: A wealthy, ultra-international micro-state with very high tech/AI salaries (AI ~€112–130k), a top-3 passport, dual citizenship retained both ways, a 5-year naturalisation path at a modest Luxembourgish-language bar, affordable study (~€0–5.2k tuition), universal healthcare and an open, stable, pro-migration politics — but constrained for this profile by a tiny single-university job market, a trilingual (Luxembourgish/French/German) reality, extreme housing costs, a small Muslim/Bangladeshi community and no in-country visa mission in Dhaka. Derived overall sits marginally above the 60% inclusion line.

job-market.summary: Among the highest tech/AI salaries in Europe in a fast-growing sector, but the domestic market is tiny and trilingual, with intense competition.

pros.text: Very high pay: AI engineers ~€112k–130k and software engineers ~€85k–130k; tech contributes ~8% of GDP with 27,000+ high-skilled jobs created since 2020 (15% growth).
EU Blue Card access is straightforward, and cross-border commuting to France/Germany/Belgium widens options.

cons.text: The domestic market is tiny (population ~660k) — limited depth to place two tech earners.
Multilingualism (English/French/German) is commonly expected, and competition for roles is intense.

links.title: Nucamp — most in-demand tech jobs in Luxembourg (2025)

visa-access.summary: Modest funds and an open posture, but Luxembourg has no mission in Dhaka, so applications route via a neighbouring/third-country mission.

pros.text: Funds requirements are modest and Luxembourg is an open, pro-migration country with no Bangladesh-specific restrictions.

cons.text: Luxembourg has no embassy/visa centre in Bangladesh, so the long-stay visa must be processed via a neighbouring or third-country mission.

citizenship.summary: A strong path: dual citizenship retained both ways, a top-3 passport, and a 5-year naturalisation requiring only a modest Luxembourgish-language test (A2 speaking / B1 listening) plus a civic course.

pros.text: Dual citizenship is allowed (Luxembourg is on Bangladesh's DNC list), and naturalisation needs only 5 years' residence with a modest Sproochentest (A2 speaking / B1 listening) and the free 'Vivre ensemble' course.
The Luxembourg passport ranks joint 3rd on Henley 2026 (~186 destinations).

cons.text: Luxembourgish is a niche language with limited daily use, so the A2 speaking requirement still demands dedicated study.

links.title: Étude — Luxembourg citizenship and the Sproochentest (2026)

post-study-work.summary: Graduates get a 9–12 month job-search/business permit (€10,000 funds, €80 fee) converting to a salaried/Blue Card permit, available to each spouse.

pros.text: Graduates can stay 9–12 months to find a job or start a business (€10,000 funds, €80 fee), converting to a salaried/Blue Card permit via an ADEM declaration; each spouse qualifies.

cons.text: The window is short and the tiny market limits placement options.

links.title: Guichet.lu — staying after studies to find a job (official)

spouse-family.summary: Blue Card families get work rights and free multilingual schooling, and if both spouses study each holds an independent track.

pros.text: EU Blue Card holders' families get labour-market access, children attend free multilingual public school, and if both spouses study each holds their own permit.

cons.text: Family migration carries income/housing conditions, costly given Luxembourg's housing prices.

msc-study.summary: Affordable English-taught master's at the (single) highly international University of Luxembourg, but the catalogue is small.

pros.text: University of Luxembourg master's tuition is low (~€0–5,200/yr), mostly English-taught, with over half of students international.
A Bangladeshi BSc-CSE is admissible, and a master qualifies for the post-study job-search permit.

cons.text: There is only one university with a small CS/AI catalogue, limiting programme choice.

links.title: University of Luxembourg — bachelor & master admissions (official)

pr-pathway.summary: Long-term residence is reachable after 5 years, eased by high salaries clearing income bars, with a modest language requirement.

pros.text: Long-term residence is reachable after 5 years, and high tech salaries easily clear income requirements.

cons.text: A modest language/integration requirement applies, and the route runs through standard EU long-term-residence conditions.

income-cost.summary: Europe's highest tech salaries are partly eaten by Europe's most expensive housing, leaving a strong but cost-squeezed surplus.

pros.text: Very high salaries support strong savings and remittance, even after tax.

cons.text: Housing is among the most expensive in Europe, badly squeezing both the study phase and early career; many commute from cheaper neighbouring countries.

links.title: Mastersportal — tuition & living costs in Luxembourg (2026)

healthcare.summary: Universal CNS coverage for workers/residents with high quality (and easy cross-border options), and a wealthy welfare net.

pros.text: The CNS gives workers and residents universal coverage (students enrol), with high-quality care and easy access to top facilities in neighbouring countries.
A wealthy state underpins a strong welfare and family-benefit system.

cons.text: Students must arrange enrolment/insurance on arrival before coverage begins.

culture-language.summary: Ultra-international (47% foreign-born) with English widely spoken and a tolerant climate, though the trilingual reality and niche Luxembourgish add friction.

pros.text: One of the world's most international societies (~47% foreign-born) where English is widely usable, with a tolerant, diverse climate and strong livability.

cons.text: Daily life and administration juggle Luxembourgish/French/German, and citizenship needs the niche Luxembourgish language.

safety-law.summary: Very safe with top-tier rule of law and a tolerant, well-governed environment.

pros.text: Very low crime, top-tier rule of law and reliable, low-corruption institutions in a wealthy, well-run state.
A diverse, tolerant society with strong women's safety.

cons.text: Limited track record of large visible-minority communities relative to bigger states.

links.title: World Justice Project — Rule of Law Index 2025

politics.summary: Stable, centrist and notably pro-migration (in a country that is ~47% foreign-born), with a marginal far-right — among the most welcoming climates in the set.

pros.text: Stable and centrist, with immigration structurally normal (≈47% of residents are foreign-born) and only a marginal far-right (ADR), making it one of the most welcoming climates in this set.

cons.text: EU-wide migration tightening still shapes the broader policy environment.

tax.summary: Progressive personal tax (0–42% plus surtax and rising social contributions) on very high salaries, with an inpatriate regime and uncertain Bangladesh treaty.

pros.text: An inpatriate (impatriés) tax regime offers relief to qualifying inbound employees, and very high gross salaries cushion the burden.

cons.text: Personal tax is progressive to 42% plus a solidarity surtax and rising pension contributions (to 8.5% in 2026), and no Luxembourg–Bangladesh treaty is confirmed.

muslim-diaspora.summary: A small but officially recognised Muslim community (~3–6%) with six mosques and imported halal, but a negligible Bangladeshi-specific presence.

pros.text: Islam is an officially recognised (and state-supported, via the Shoura) religion; there are six mosques and halal food is widely available (imported from France/Belgium).

cons.text: The Muslim community is small (~3–6%) and the Bangladeshi-specific community and South-Asian ecosystem are negligible.
